content.text: Every Cowork task is transparent: as the agentic engine works, it records each tool call, each file it reads, and each artifact it writes in a live
Steps View

. Reviewing those steps as they happen — and evaluating the final deliverable against the goal you defined — is the core human-in-the-loop skill in Cowork. Knowing what to look for turns task review from a passive sign-off into active quality control.
The Task Review Lifecycle

attrs.alt: Cowork task review lifecycle: five phases (Task Submitted, Engine Planning, Execution Steps, Final Output, Deliverable Review) with a Steps View panel and a Review Actions panel
attrs.title: Cowork Task Review: Steps and Deliverable Lifecycle

content.text: Cowork task review happens at two distinct moments:
during execution
(watching the steps view in real time) and
after completion
(evaluating the final deliverable against the goal you defined). The actions available at each moment differ.
Anatomy of a Step Entry

Each step entry surfaces four pieces of information: the tool the engine invoked, the input it supplied to that tool, the output the tool returned, and the step status. Reading these four elements tells you whether the engine is working on the right problem with the right data.
Field
What it tells you
What to check
Tool name
Which capability was invoked (read_file, run_python, search_web, write_file, etc.)
Is this a tool you expected? Unexpected tools signal a misunderstood goal
Input
The file path, query, or code the engine passed to the tool
Does the path or query match the data you intended? Wrong paths produce wrong output
Output
The result returned: row counts, parsed structure, error messages, file sizes
Does the count or shape match what you know about your data? Zero rows is an early warning
Status icon
Spinner (running), check (complete), warning (needs attention)
Warnings do not stop the task; you decide whether to pause or cancel
Watching Steps During Execution

The Steps View updates in real time. You do not need to watch every step — most tasks run correctly. But three patterns are worth catching early, because they are cheaper to correct by cancelling a running task than by re-running after wrong output has been produced.
Pattern 1: Wrong data source
The engine reads a file or queries a connector you did not intend. This usually means the workspace contains multiple similarly-named files, or the goal did not specify the source file explicitly.

# Step seen in the view:
Tool: read_file
Input: /workspace/q3-budget.csv    # <-- wrong quarter
Output: 843 rows parsed

# Action: Cancel and revise the goal
# Add: "Use /workspace/q4-budget.csv as the source file."
Pattern 2: Unexpectedly large scope

The engine iterates over far more items than expected — hundreds of files, thousands of rows — suggesting the task will run much longer than intended or will produce unmanageably large output. Cancel early, narrow the scope in the goal, and resubmit.
Pattern 3: Loop or repetition

The steps view shows the engine calling the same tool on the same input repeatedly. This indicates the engine is stuck — often because the goal has no stopping condition. Cancel, add a count limit or a done-condition, and resubmit.
Signals That Warrant Intervention
Not every unusual step needs intervention. This table maps specific observations to the correct response.
What you see
Likely cause
Response
Wrong file path in input
Goal did not specify the exact file
Cancel; add explicit file path to goal
Unexpected tool (e.g. search_web for an internal task)
Goal wording implied external lookup
Pause and inspect; cancel if output will be wrong
Output: 0 rows or empty result
Data missing, filter too strict, or wrong date range
Cancel; verify data exists before resubmitting
Same tool called 5+ times on same input
No stopping condition; engine is looping
Cancel; add count or done-condition to goal
Warning icon on a write_file step
File already exists or write failed silently
Let run to completion; inspect the written file manually after
Mid-Task Actions: Continue, Pause, Cancel
Three controls are available while a task is running. Choosing the right one determines whether you recover quickly or wait for wrong output.
Continue
Do nothing. The task proceeds normally. Use this when the steps look correct, the tools are expected, and the data sources match. Most tasks need no intervention.
Pause

Halts execution after the current step finishes. Use this when you want to inspect an intermediate file before the engine continues, or when you see an unexpected tool and want to understand why it was selected without losing work done so far. After inspecting, you can resume or cancel.
Cancel

Stops the task immediately. Workspace files written before the cancel remain — they are not rolled back. Cancel when the task is heading in the wrong direction and continuing will not produce usable output. After cancelling, revise the goal and resubmit rather than simply rerunning the same prompt.

Cancel does not undo workspace writes. If the engine partially wrote a file before you cancelled, that partial file remains. Inspect it before resubmitting to avoid reading stale data in the next run.
Evaluating the Final Deliverable

When the task completes, the Steps View shows a final step indicating where the output was written. Evaluating the deliverable is a structured comparison between the output you received and the outcome you defined.
The five-question checklist
Correct deliverable?
Did the engine produce the specific thing you named (a CSV, a markdown report, a JSON file), or something else?
Correct format?
Does it use the format you specified (columns, headings, ranked list, table structure)?
Correct scope?
Does it cover exactly the data you scoped (right date range, right files, right topic boundary)?
Correct quantity?
If you specified a count (top 10, five examples, all rows where X), does the output contain that many items?
Spot-check a sample.
Pick 2-3 items from the output and verify them against the source data manually. If those are wrong, the rest of the output is suspect.
Post-Completion Actions: Accept, Revise, Re-run
Action
When to use
Next step
Accept
Output passes all five checklist questions
Task is done; use the deliverable
Follow-up task
Output is mostly correct but needs a targeted correction (wrong column order, missing field, one section to rewrite)
Start a new task referencing the existing output file by path; describe only the correction needed
Re-run with revised goal
Output fails multiple checklist questions or format is fundamentally wrong
Diagnose which goal property was missing and resubmit from scratch
Inspect then decide
Unsure whether the output is correct (spot-check inconclusive)
Open the workspace file and verify 3-5 more samples against source data before deciding
Using the Follow-Up Task Pattern

The follow-up task is the most common post-review action. Because Cowork persists all workspace files across tasks, you can chain tasks without re-processing the original data. The key is to reference the existing output file explicitly in the follow-up goal.

# Original task produced:
# /workspace/cost-analysis.md  (correct data, wrong column order)

# Follow-up task goal:
Read /workspace/cost-analysis.md.
Reorder the table columns so the order is:
  Cost Centre | Q4 Actual | Q4 Budget | Variance | Variance %
Write the corrected version back to /workspace/cost-analysis.md.
Do not change any values -- only reorder columns.

The follow-up task does not re-read the source CSV or re-run the analysis. It operates only on the existing output file. This keeps follow-up tasks fast and prevents re-introducing the original error.
Reading Steps to Improve Future Goals

The Steps View is also a diagnostic tool for goal refinement. When a task produces mediocre output, the steps often reveal exactly why: which tool was used on which data, and at what point the engine deviated from what you intended.
Step log observation
Goal improvement
Engine read 12 files when you wanted 1
Name the exact file path in the goal
Engine called run_python 4 times where 1 would have sufficed
Goal had multiple sub-tasks; split into sequential tasks
Engine wrote output to a path you did not specify
Add explicit output path: save to /workspace/output.csv
Engine searched the web for data you have locally
Add: Do not search the web. Use only /workspace/ files.
Connector Steps: What They Look Like

When a task uses cloud connectors (Slack, Drive, GitHub, Jira), the step entries look different from local file operations. The tool name reflects the connector, and the input shows the query or filter the engine constructed. Reviewing connector steps is especially important because the engine may interpret your goal broadly and search channels or repositories you did not intend.

# Slack connector step:
Tool: slack_search
Input: query='Q4 budget decision' channels=['#finance','#leadership'] after=2025-09-01
Output: 14 messages matched

# Drive connector step:
Tool: drive_read
Input: file_id='1Bxyz...' format=text
Output: 12,400 characters extracted

# GitHub connector step:
Tool: github_list_prs
Input: repo='acme/api' state=open labels=['bug']
Output: 7 pull requests

When reviewing connector steps, check: Does the query match the intent? Are the channels, repositories, or file IDs what you expected? A Slack search scoped to the wrong channel, or a Drive query returning files from a shared drive you did not intend, produces output that looks plausible but contains the wrong data.
Connector step scope checklist
Slack searches:
confirm the channels list; an unscoped search queries all channels the connector can see, which may include private channels with sensitive data
Drive reads:
verify the file ID or folder path matches what you intended; IDs are not human-readable so cross-reference the filename shown in the output field
GitHub queries:
check the repo name and branch; a search scoped to the wrong repo returns unrelated data without error
Jira queries:
confirm the project key and issue type filter match your task; JQL is interpreted literally, so a missing filter can return all open issues across the instance
Verifying Intermediate Files in the Workspace

For long-running tasks — multi-step pipelines that produce several intermediate files before writing the final deliverable — you can pause after each write step and verify the intermediate file before the next stage runs. This catches data errors before they propagate through the pipeline.

# After engine wrote /workspace/summary.md, open and spot-check:

# Check 1: Does the file exist and have reasonable size?
$ ls -lh /workspace/summary.md
-rw-r--r--  1 user  staff   8.2K Jun 30 11:42 /workspace/summary.md

# Check 2: Read the first 20 lines
$ head -20 /workspace/summary.md
# Q4 Cost Analysis -- Finance Department
Generated: 2025-06-30
Source: /workspace/q4-budget.csv (1,204 rows)

| Cost Centre | Q4 Actual | Q4 Budget | Variance |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Engineering | 482,100   | 510,000   | -5.5%    |
| Marketing   | 391,200   | 380,000   | +2.9%    |
...

# Check 3: Verify a sample row against the source CSV
$ grep 'Engineering' /workspace/q4-budget.csv | awk -F, '{sum+=$3} END{print sum}'
482100.00
# Matches the summary row -- spot-check passes

Three things to verify in any intermediate file: that it exists and has a non-trivial size (an empty or near-zero file indicates a failed write), that the structure matches what you expected (right columns, right format), and that at least one value spot-checks correctly against the source. If all three pass, resume the task.
Step Count as an Efficiency Signal

The number of steps a task takes is a proxy for how precisely the goal was defined. Vague goals produce high step counts because the engine must explore before it can act. Precise goals produce low step counts because the engine can go straight to the answer.

# Run 1 (vague goal): 18 steps, 4 min 12 sec
Steps: read_file x 6, run_python x 8, write_file x 4
# Engine tried multiple approaches; rewrote the output 4 times

# Run 2 (precise goal with column list and output path): 6 steps, 58 sec
Steps: read_file x 1, run_python x 4, write_file x 1
# Engine went straight to the answer; step count fell by 67%

Use step count as a calibration signal across runs of the same class of task. If a similar task produced fewer steps last time and better output, the current goal is likely underspecified. Trace where the divergence happened and add the missing constraint.
Error Recovery: When a Step Fails

A step failure (a tool call that returns an error rather than a result) does not always stop the task. The engine may attempt a retry or an alternative approach. Watch for two failure modes in the step log.
Recoverable failure

The engine retries with a corrected input (different file path, adjusted query, smaller batch) and succeeds. You will see the failure step followed immediately by a successful repeat of the same tool. No action needed; the engine self-corrected.
Unrecoverable failure

The engine retries multiple times and each attempt fails, or it proceeds with degraded data (for example, skipping rows that caused an error). The final deliverable is incomplete. In the step log, look for repeated warning icons on the same tool, or a write_file step immediately after a cluster of failures — the engine tried to write what it had rather than what you asked for.
Failure type
What the step log shows
Response
File not found
Warning icon; engine tries alternative path or fallback file
Cancel; upload or move the file to /workspace/ and resubmit
Connector auth error
Error in output field; no retry (auth cannot self-correct)
Cancel; re-authenticate the connector in settings, then resubmit
Parse error on data file
Warning; engine proceeds with partial rows
Let complete; inspect output row count against known total and re-run if coverage is below threshold
Timeout on long-running code step
Warning; engine may retry with smaller batch
Cancel; split the data into smaller batches in the goal
Keeping a Review Log

For recurring tasks — weekly reports, daily data pulls, periodic audits — keeping a lightweight review log pays compounding dividends. Each time you note what you checked and what decision you made, you build a reference that makes the next review faster and catches systematic goal-definition problems that only become visible across multiple runs.

A review log does not need to be elaborate. A plain text or markdown file in the workspace with three sections — what you observed during execution, what you verified in the deliverable, and what action you took — is enough.

Checklist: Task Review Log
==========================
Task: Q4 cost analysis
Goal version: 2 (revised after first run)
Date: 2025-06-30

During execution:
[ ] Data source correct: q4-budget.csv (1,204 rows)  -- YES
[ ] Tools expected: read_file, run_python, write_file -- YES
[ ] No unexpected connectors queried               -- YES
[ ] No loop/repeat pattern in steps                -- YES

Final deliverable:
[ ] File at /workspace/cost-analysis.md            -- YES (8.2 KB)
[ ] Format: markdown table with 5 columns          -- YES
[ ] Scope: Q4 only, Finance dept only              -- YES
[ ] Count: all 12 cost centres present             -- YES (12 rows)
[ ] Spot-check: Engineering row matches source     -- YES

Decision: ACCEPT
Notes: Step count fell from 18 to 6 vs first run; goal revision worked

Over several runs, the log reveals patterns: which goals consistently produce high step counts, which data sources are frequently wrong, which deliverable fields require follow-up tasks. That information directly improves goal definitions for the next run.
Review Depth by Task Risk

Not every task warrants the same review depth. A task that generates a draft document you will edit before sharing has a low cost of error; a task that writes back to a shared data store or sends a message to a Slack channel has a high cost of error. Calibrate review depth to the consequence of getting it wrong.
Task category
Risk level
Recommended review depth
Draft document, internal summary, analysis report
Low — you review before distributing
Spot-check 2-3 items; skim format; accept or minor follow-up
Data transformation or ETL output
Medium — downstream systems depend on it
Full five-question checklist; verify row counts and spot-check 5-10 values
Message sent to Slack, email drafted, Jira comment written
High — visible to others immediately
Watch execution live; pause before the send step to read the draft; verify recipients and tone before resuming
File overwrite on a shared Drive or repo
High — overwrites may be irreversible
Pause before every write step; always write to a /workspace/ draft first, then manually copy after review
Summary

Reviewing steps and deliverables is a two-phase skill: watch the Steps View during execution for wrong data sources, unexpected tools, and loop patterns — cancel early when you spot them. After completion, run the five-question checklist against your original outcome definition. If the deliverable passes, accept it. If it needs a targeted fix, use a follow-up task referencing the existing output file. If it fails fundamentally, diagnose which goal property was missing and resubmit. The step log is also a diagnostic artifact: reading it reveals the root cause of mediocre output and tells you exactly how to tighten the goal for the next run.
